text.title: AI-Assisted Spec Writing Workflow and Principles
text.summary: SDD-AI workflow phases, human-AI collaboration model, supported formats, and LLM selection guide.

rule.statement: Write specifications before implementation begins — capture expected behaviors, edge cases, and constraints upfront.
rule.rationale: AI-generated code quality directly correlates with specification quality. Vague intent produces hallucinated requirements. A well-specified feature with explicit edge cases and acceptance criteria produces accurate, consistent implementation.

rule.statement: Require human approval after every AI-generated specification before implementation tasks are created.
rule.rationale: AI lacks domain context, organizational priorities, and business rule nuance. AI-generated specs contain plausible-sounding but incorrect requirements that only a domain expert can identify. Without a human gate, these errors propagate into implementation and compound.

rule.statement: State non-goals explicitly in every specification — LLMs will generate requirements for topics not excluded.
rule.rationale: LLMs attempt to be helpful by inferring what "should" be included. An auth feature spec without an explicit "this spec does not cover password reset" will often contain a password reset FR that was never intended.

rule.statement: Keep individual spec files under 15,000 tokens — coherence degrades in very long specs even within large context windows.
rule.rationale: Research confirms 150-200 requirement limit per spec call maintains model consistency. Beyond this, LLMs begin contradicting earlier requirements and losing constraint awareness.

list.item: INTENT: human describes problem, constraints, context
SPEC: AI + human collaboration — AI structures, asks questions, drafts; human validates
PLAN: AI generates implementation-plan.md from approved spec
EXECUTION: AI agent executes tasks from plan
REVIEW: human verifies implementation against acceptance criteria
Intent capture: human describes problem; AI structures thoughts and asks clarifying questions
Requirement extraction: human validates completeness; AI generates from context
Edge cases: human confirms business relevance; AI enumerates technical possibilities
Acceptance criteria: human approves and verifies testability; AI drafts Given-When-Then
Validation: human final approval; AI checks consistency and completeness
Structured Markdown (spec.md): technical specs, internal docs — default for SDD
YAML/JSON: machine-readable specs, OpenAPI, JSON Schema
Natural language: stakeholder communication, early-stage exploration
EARS notation: "When [trigger], the [system]..." — formal enterprise requirements
Claude Opus/Sonnet 4.x: complex system specs, long documents, 200k context — default choice
GPT-4.1/5.x: general specs, polyglot projects, balanced versatility
Gemini 2.5/3 Pro: massive context (1M+), large-scale projects, codebase analysis
o1/o3: formal specs, complex dependency chains, advanced reasoning requirements

text.reference: LLM prompt template for initial spec generation
LLM prompt template for spec review: completeness, conflicts, testability
